1.description: Elasticity of investment with respect to equity volatility. This figure presents the elasticity of investment with respect to equity volatility over time and across firms using estimates from the regressions with interaction terms. In each quarter, we generate five cutoffs in the cross section of log credit spread:{p10,p30,p50,p70,p90}$\lbrace p_{10}, p_{30}, p_{50}, p_{70}, p_{90}\rbrace$. Using the estimates in column (7) of TableVIonlog[I/K]i,t=β1logσi,te+β2logcsi,t+γlogσi,te×logcsi,t+ηi+λt+εi,t$\log [I/K]_{i,t} = \beta _1 \log \sigma ^{e}_{i,t}+\beta _2\log cs_{i,t}+\gamma \log \sigma ^{e}_{i,t}\times \log cs_{i,t}+\eta _i+\lambda _t+\epsilon _{i,t}$, the elasticity at each cutoff point is computed asβ1+γpn$\beta _1+\gamma p_n$,n=10,30,50,70,90$n=10,30,50,70,90$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Elasticity of investment with respect to credit spread. This figure presents the elasticity of investment with respect to credit spread over time and across firms using estimates from the regressions with interaction terms. In each quarter, we generate five cutoffs in the cross section of log equity volatility:{p10,p30,p50,p70,p90}$\lbrace p_{10}, p_{30}, p_{50}, p_{70}, p_{90}\rbrace$. Using the estimates in column (7) of TableVIonlog[I/K]i,t=β1logσi,te+β2logcsi,t+γlogσi,te×logcsi,t+ηi+λt+εi,t$\log [I/K]_{i,t} = \beta _1 \log \sigma ^e_{i,t}+\beta _2\log cs_{i,t}+\gamma \log \sigma ^e_{i,t}\times \log cs_{i,t}+\eta _i+\lambda _t+\epsilon _{i,t}$, the elasticity at each cutoff point is computed asβ2+γpn$\beta _2+\gamma p_n$,n=10,30,50,70,90$n=10,30,50,70,90$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Optimal investment with log-normal distribution. The left panel shows the level of investmenti$i$as a function of credit spreadscs$cs$for different levels of asset volatilityσ$\sigma$, while the right panel shows the level of investmenti$i$as a function of asset volatilityσ$\sigma$for different levels of credit spreadscs$cs$. The adjustment cost function is given byϕ(i)=iγ$\phi (i) = i^\gamma$withγ=2$\gamma =2$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Optimal investment with log-normal distribution. The left panel shows the level of investmenti$i$as a function of leverageb$b$for different levels of asset volatilityσ$\sigma$, while the right panel shows the level of investmenti$i$as a function of asset volatilityσ$\sigma$for different levels of leverageb$b$. The adjustment cost function is given byϕ(i)=iγ$\phi (i) = i^\gamma$withγ=2$\gamma =2$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Sign of wedges for log-normal and log-normal mixture. The panels show the sign of the wedges of Proposition3in the (cs,σ$cs,\sigma$)-space for the log-normal distribution (left) and a log-normal mixture distribution (right). The mixture distribution is a mixture of two log-normal distributions drawn with 50% probability with parameters(μ1,σ̂)$(\mu _1,\hat{\sigma })$and(μ2,σ̂)$(\mu _2,\hat{\sigma })$such that the unconditional mean ofz$z$is one and the standard deviation ofz$z$isσ$\sigma$. We setσ̂=0.2$\hat{\sigma }=0.2$in this example.

6.description: Impulse responses of investment to shocks to asset volatility and credit spreads. This figure plots the impulse responses of investment to an orthogonalized one standard deviation shock to asset volatility and credit spread. We use the value-weighted average of asset volatilityσi,t$\sigma _{i,t}$, credit spreadcsi,t$cs_{i,t}$, and investment rate[I/K]i,t$[I/K]_{i,t}$to generate the corresponding aggregate time series for asset volatility (σ$\sigma$), credit spreads (cs$cs$), and investment rate (I/K$I/K$). The VAR is estimated using four lags of each endogenous variable. Panels (A) and (B) correspond to the recursive ordering (cs$cs$,σ$\sigma$,I/K$I/K$). Panels (C) and (D) correspond to the recursive ordering (σ$\sigma$,cs$cs$,I/K$I/K$). The shaded bands represent the 95% confidence interval.
